exchanges.question: You've mentioned your court drama where two friends rip each other apart, and you talked about building a production company to compete with Warner Bros. Let's get specific about how you think about story. When you start developing a new story idea, what comes first for you - a character, a situation, a theme, a visual, a dialogue? Where does a story begin in your head?
exchanges.response: Naku edhyna inspiring scene na thought process add avvali, when viewing suits, I was very impressed of the characters. So I thought when two people argue and then they are arguing for one another, but they don't know that, they can't reveal it. Now inorder to win the case, they have go to the length where the opposite guy loose his ccolness and fail the case. Adhi avvali ante both sides sensitive parts pettali, one of the hero mothers old profession is a prostitute. What are they fighting for, normal ga kakunda, I've started it like a cheating case between big people and I want to convert it into a murder case, this is where people should jump out of the chairs. climax chala important naku, movie nundi bayataki vellemundhu, adhe gurthu untadhi. Taruvatha cheating case ki murder case ki common ga oka hot assasin pettanu. Now I have to chain them together with interesting points using the models, research, books, movies etc.

exchanges.question: You said you chain plot points together using 'models, research, books, movies.' Walk me through that. When you sit down to work on this court drama, what does your actual process look like? Do you have a whiteboard, index cards, notes on your phone? How do you organize a story that has cheating, murder, and an assassin connecting them all?
exchanges.response: I write in my Google notes about every inspiration that I get. so basically if I watch a scene, that thought lead me to another vision and that to another. So basically if I have a starting point, I don't know which gives that, but it leads to me a different vision. That's where I write it down. Then I start research on how courts work if this is a high-level cheating case, which court should I go? What are the sections on that cheating case how many should be involved and what are the interesting points of a high-level celebrity cheating case? I will also brainstorm with my team, watch movies and stich together my inspirations

exchanges.question: You mentioned 'brainstorm with my team.' Who is this team? Is it your sister, friends with film backgrounds, or people you've assembled specifically for this? And when you brainstorm together, how does that work - do you pitch ideas and they react, or is it more collaborative where they add their own threads?
exchanges.response: right now, I'm a producer and the only asset I have is my sister, and I have a friend on my team, so they pitch ideas. Na scripts, stories nene rasukuntunna, vallavi vinta prasthuthaniki.

exchanges.question: You write your own scripts and listen to their pitches. When your sister or friend pitches an idea to you, what makes you say 'yes, let's develop this' versus 'not right now'? What are you filtering for - is it the concept, the commercial potential, or something else entirely?
exchanges.response: I need vision details, and depth of character development in any story I listen. Kotthaga fresh topic aina, interesting topic aina, ledha new perpective for an old topic aina, I admire it. I need to see the passion in their approach. I will I always have my set of questions for their pitch, the question includes: Em reference teesukoni raasav? what inspired you, what is your depth in character development? , my perspective of their story related questions etc. I'll provide all the help from my side to boost the story, if I see a vision, considering all this, I'm good to go.

exchanges.question: You said earlier 'climax chala important naku' - the ending is what stays with people. Walk me through your court drama's climax. Without spoiling everything, what's the feeling you want the audience to leave with? Is it shock, satisfaction, heartbreak, or something more complex?
exchanges.response: I want to leave few clue scenes in the middle of the movie, of course they find entertaining, but the audience shouldn't know what they're properly for, climax ki occhesariki they know the backstory of the two lawyer friends, audience knows which words and scenarios are so sensitive for the friends, eppudythe case gelavadniki personal ga comments chesi, hurt chesi, judge ki sorry cheppi, ila continue avuthado, people should be having a mixture of shock and awe - something like stunned disbelief. They should feel "I can't believe they just said that. Almost a gasp inducing tension." With all these flowing, after all the argument, they start to look in details and they put together the story and finally realise that it is actually murder, not a cheating case.

exchanges.question: You mentioned 'judge ki sorry cheppi' - they apologize to the judge but keep attacking. That's specific courtroom texture. When you write dialogue - especially confrontational scenes like this - do you write out loud, act it out, or is it purely on paper? How do you know when dialogue sounds real versus written?
exchanges.response: I'll first write a draft on paper and refine it with model and by then, I'll prepare a team who will work on this project. I'll have everyone in the writers room with the draft dialogues, and then we start discussing it and act to refine more dialogues and gather more information.

exchanges.question: You mentioned Suits as an inspiration for the court drama concept. Beyond that, what films or shows have genuinely shaped how you think about storytelling? Not just what you enjoy watching, but work that changed how you approach structure, character, or dialogue?
exchanges.response: English: Suits, Friends, Good Doctor, Game of Thrones, Prison Break, You. Japanese: Naruto, One Piece, Attack on Titan, Solo Leveling. Telugu: Jersey, Ee Nagaraniki Em Aindhi, Pilla Zamindar, Leader. Hindi: Zindagi Na Milegi Dobara, Ae Dil Hai Mushkil.

exchanges.question: Last one for film - looking at that list, there's long-form anime arcs, tight Hindi emotional dramas, Telugu mass entertainers, American plot-driven shows. If you had to pick ONE type of story you want to be known for making - your signature style that audiences associate with your production company - what would it be? What gap in current cinema do you want to fill?
exchanges.response: I want to make emotional romantic comedies and I'd appreciate if my production company is known for it.
